ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Banh Canh Cua or Mee Tai Mak crab noodle soup. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (noodle-based soup) is the closest available category. No specific code exists for Vietnamese-style crab tapioca noodle soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for this specific dish. SNOMED CT does not provide granular coverage of Southeast Asian noodle soup preparations.

main_ingredients: mee tai mak (rice flour noodles / tapioca noodles)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Crab Meat Mee Tai Mak Noodle Soup weighs approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, crab meat, pork components, and broth.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: Noodle soup with seafood and pork (composite estimate); cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asian noodle soups
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Banh Canh Cua exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for crab meat (FDC ID 174202), pork belly (FDC ID 168375), pork leg/ham (FDC ID 167903), and rice/tapioca noodles (FDC ID 168917), combined in approximate proportions typical of a Vietnamese-style crab noodle soup (noodles ~35%, broth ~40%, crab meat ~10%, pork components ~15%). Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for the assembled dish as served. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice noodles and analogous noodle soup preparations — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Banh Canh Cua. GI estimated at 55 (low) based on the GI of rice noodles and tapioca-based noodles (GI range 53–61 per University of Sydney GI Database) as the primary carbohydrate source. The protein and fat content from crab meat and pork components moderates the overall glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 51.75) / 100 ≈ 28.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 11.5 × 4.5 = 51.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from broth, fish sauce, and processed pork components
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single 450 g bowl provides approximately 2160 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice and tapioca noodles as the primary carbohydrate source, with a low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard bowl provides approximately 52 g of carbohydrate with a low GI of 55, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 28.5; portion control and pairing with additional protein or non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from pork belly, pork leg, and broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption due to the combined saturated fat contribution from pork belly and pork leg and the high sodium load from the broth; choosing leaner pork cuts and requesting less broth can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab Meat Mee Tai Mak Noodle Soup, known in Vietnamese as Bánh Canh Cua, is a hearty Southeast Asian noodle soup dish served in Singapore, particularly at Vietnamese-influenced restaurants. The dish features mee tai mak — short, thick, cylindrical noodles made from rice flour or a rice-tapioca flour blend — submerged in a rich, savoury broth prepared from pork bones or seafood stock. The soup is topped with generous portions of crab meat, pork ham, pork belly, and pork leg, creating a complex flavour profile that is simultaneously sweet from the crab, umami-rich from the pork, and deeply savoury from the broth seasoned with fish sauce and aromatics such as garlic and shallots. The noodles have a distinctive soft, slightly chewy, and slippery texture that absorbs the broth well. Clinically, this dish is notable for its high sodium content (approximately 480 mg per 100 g, or over 2100 mg per bowl), which is a significant concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The low glycemic index of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of around 28.5 per bowl make it a food requiring portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish provides meaningful protein from crab and pork (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g) and moderate fat (3.1 g per 100 g), with saturated fat primarily from pork belly. It is not suitable for halal or vegetarian diets due to pork content.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Crab Meat Mee Tai Mak Noodle Soup (Banh Canh Cua) was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for crab meat, pork belly, pork leg/ham, and rice/tapioca noodles in proportions typical of this dish. GI is estimated from analogous rice and tapioca noodle data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component entries for crab meat (FDC ID 174202), pork belly (FDC ID 168375), pork leg/ham (FDC ID 167903), and rice/tapioca noodles (FDC ID 168917), weighted by approximate proportions in the assembled dish. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as vitamin D is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimation; the value is genuinely absent from the source data. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as vitamin E is not reported consistently across the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimation; the value is genuinely absent from the source data. selenium_mcg is -1 as selenium is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component sources used for this composite estimation; the value is genuinely absent from the source data. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero) as the primary ingredients — crab, pork, and rice/tapioca noodles — do not contain industrially produced trans fats; any naturally occurring trans fat from pork is below detection threshold (trace). The dish is not halal due to pork content. GI is estimated at 55 based on analogous rice and tapioca noodle data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Banh Canh Cua was identified in the literature. Sodium content is notably high due to broth and fish sauce; individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease should exercise caution. This record was sourced from GrabFood Singapore (1 restaurant: Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road); the restaurant data is preserved for provenance but nutrient values are not derived from the delivery platform.
